The patches of tree damage were most evident right before the deepest tornado debris signatures were visible on KMOB near the beginning of the track and again near Gavin Hamilton Rd. The tornado evidently weakened as it occluded northwest and the tornado debris signature dissipates not long after this point.

A brief EF1 tornado with max wind speeds of 90 MPH touched down just north of the MS river east of Darrow, LA. It damaged four well built, newer, homes. Approximately 20% of each roof was damaged, removed or peeled back. Front porch pillars were moved or ripped away, fences damaged and one garage door blown in. Splatter on the east-northeast sides of the homes was noted as well as multiple pieces of wood embedded into the ground. Additional analysis via high resolution satellite imagery confirmed that the tornado began on the other side of the Mississippi River bank near Gautreau Road and LA Hwy 18 where a few trees were damaged and an outbuilding was visibly destroyed.

An EF-0 tornado with max winds of 75 MPH touched down briefly in Gramercy, LA starting near Hwy 3125 and continuing northeast. A few homes, along Millet Dr had minor roof damage and most homes in the neighborhood having fence damage. Debris was noted to be lofted into the back field as well as some splatter seen on the east-northeast side of homes. The tornado is estimated to have ended before US Hwy 61 though it's possible the tornado continued into the Maurepas Swamp Wildlife Management Area. The continuation  of this path was not supported by radar nor high resolution satellite imagery, and was inaccessible to ground survey.

A tornadic waterspout of unknown intensity came ashore on the northern side of Lake Lery causing a broad swath of damage to open marsh which was collocated with strong rotation and a weak tornado debris signature on KLIX radar.

An EF-0 tornado touched down near Middle Colyell Creek where trees were visibly damaged on high resolution satellite imagery. The tornado continued to Springfield Road where it tore a car port off a house and lifted most of the roof off. It then ripped part of the roof off a nearby building and peeled back the tin on the porch. Tin was peeled back on a building across the street. All debris was thrown around a quarter of a mile or more to the northeast some of this damage could be seen in the field near Coon Creek Road on high resolution satellite imagery. Power lines were also reported down on Rafe Ballard Road. An additional swath of trees were damaged in the field north of Coon Creek Road. The tornado weakened thereafter and lifted over dense forested areas west of Corbin Road.

A storm likely spawned a waterspout near the northshore of Lake Pontchartrain and moved onshore as a tornado near Guste Island around 2:50 AM CDT. The tornado tracked to the north-northwest across mainly uninhabited swamp lands about two miles west of the Tchefuncte River. Sporadic softwood and hardwood tree damage was noted on the eastern shore of the inlet. Additional damage was noted in high resolution satellite imagery which depicted numerous uprooted trees that were corroborated by ground surveys. A small camp was also hit by the RFD or outskirts of this tornado and was destroyed as seen in high resolution satellite imagery afterward.

The tornado was also confirmed by KLIX radar data with a tornado debris signature evident as it was crossing the inlet at 2:53 AM CDT. The tornado quickly lifted around 2:55 AM as it approached the northern shore of this inlet.

A tornado touched down near Hwy 190 and Hollycrest Blvd in Covington where damage was first noted in the Hollycrest Plaza Shopping Mall. A portion of the north-facing exterior wall of the building complex collapsed. There was also minor roof damage and the fence in the back parking lot was blown over. Sporadic tree damage (small pines uprooted and large branches snapped) was noted along 5th Ave, Soell Dr, and Mire Dr as the tornado tracked to the east. The end point was extended to the location of the thunderstorm wind damage in the neighborhood to the east which was supported by additional tree damage seen on satellite and a tornado debris signature on KLIX which continued to just southwest of the St. Tammany Parish Airport.

An EF-0 tornado embedded within a line of storms touched down over parts of rural northwest Jackson County. Only patchy minor tree damage was observed on high resolution satellite imagery, but was collocated with an apparent tornado debris signature on KMOB radar.

A brief EF-0 tornado touched down near Harleston, MS in Jackson County causing minor tree damage visible on high resolution satellite imagery. Estimated max wind speeds were near 70 mph and the damage was collocated with a tornado debris signature on the KMOB radar.

A brief tornado touched down on the southeast side of Belle Terre Playground in Marrero, LA and tracked northeast. A shed was flipped near the starting point with no other damage found to the southwest. Another shed with no connection points was rolled on it's side on Adam Drive with minor roof damage noted to the home. A few other houses with minor roof damage were observed along the track to the northeast. On Victorian Drive, a small shed that was tied down with a few straps and posts in concrete was thrown northeast with other debris thrown northwest. Minor roof damage was also noted in this area. No damage was observed in the trees at the end of Marine Street and no damage was found farther to the northeast.

A large, embedded supercell moved across Dauphin Island during the early morning hours of May 10th. NWS ground survey crew assessed the damage that occurred across the island finding mostly sporadic damage without any coherent signs of convergent damage, something we look for when it comes to determining whether or not the damage was associated with a tornado or straight-line winds. Given this, the determination has been that intense straight-line winds up to 90mph occurred across the northern parts of the island. This damage consisted most notably of a collapsed small cell tower. Capt'n Snappers restaurant had windows broken, their door pushed in, and an AC unit tossed off a wooden crate it was sitting on. A few boats on the marina were pulled out into the water resulting in some damage to them. Across the rest of the area, mostly small to large tree limbs were taken down with a few uprooted and snapped trees scattered across the island.

The damage path of an EF-0 tornado was identified via high resolution satellite over rural dense forests southwest of Wilkinson, MS. The tornado caused visible tree damage to tree tops near Fords Creek and Hackett Ford Road before causing a more notable swath of tree damage to the east of inaccessible areas. The maximum sustained winds are estimated at 85 mph due to what are likely several uprooted trees and large, high snaps of trees on high resolution satellite imagery.

A well-defined tornado damage path was identified in the marsh grass of lower Plaquemines Parish, just east of Pilottown, LA. The tornado moved to the northeast across Joe Brown Pass where a 25-yard-wide swath of marsh grass was notably damaged as well as a couple of trees uprooted along the bank. This was the only damage indicator identified and is the basis for the rating of this tornado. The tornado continued northeast intermittently causing damage to marsh grass across the Delta National Wildlife Refuge. North of Octave Pass, the tornado damage path in the marsh grass widened to around 50 yards which matched the increase in strength of the rotation aloft on KHDC radar. The tornado damage path became more difficult as it paralleled Main Pass and the end point is estimated off the last confident point of damage to the marsh. The rating of this tornado was not possible given the lack of damage indicators.

The track of an EF-2 tornado began near Hwy 569 northeast of Liberty, MS in Amite County. High resolution satellite imagery identified an increasingly intense and widening swath of tree damage extending to Clark Rd. Numerous trees were snapped spanning 225 yards in width at Clark Rd and the tornado continued to intensify and widen as it reached Shaw and Wells Rd. On Wells Rd, the tornado tore most of the roof structure off of a single family home and also completely destroyed a 60x40 metal building bolted to a concrete slab. Also in this area, nearly every tree near the center of the path was snapped. This area of damage was consistent with maximum winds of around 130 mph. It was also widest in this area, reaching a maximum width of 0.4 miles. The tornado gradually began weakening as it continued traveling east northeast snapping and uprooting trees along its path. Then it crossed into Pike County near Slay Rd. As it continued moving east-northeast it snapped and uprooted numerous hard and soft wood trees rated EF-1, some of which caused damage to homes as they fell. A broad swath of tree damage roughly half a mile wide was associated with this tornado due to several subtornadic vortices and rear flank downdrafts that produced swaths of tree damage visible on high resolution satellite imagery. This also made identifying the location of the parent vortex track harder to discern as the tornado approached Interstate 55 and Veterans Blvd in McComb, MS. On both sides of the interstate, damage was primarily to trees, with several trees snapped or uprooted along the path rated EF-1. Again, falling trees caused some structural damage to homes. The tornado track began to turn more northeastward near Northwest and Marion Ave. Broad swaths of tree damage continued into McComb which was deemed to be associated with a large surge in straight-line winds on the southern flank of the dissipating tornado. The tornado gradually weakened and dissipated between John D Shaw St and Robb St. This concluded a 20-mile-long damage path in which the tornado was on the ground for 24 minutes.

Several buildings in Camille Village Apartments sustained minor damage, including siding peeled. Three buildings had their AC units blown over or support structure compromised. One of the AC units was not found.
An awning attached to the front of one of these building was completely destroyed. Roofing and awning material was lofted and found about 1000 yards to the northwest past a fence and area of trees.

The tornado first touched down just west of Orchard Dr, north of Howells Ferry Rd. It continued northeast and moved across the Princeton Woods subdivision. After that it crossed Moffett Rd near University Ave and came to an end. No damage was noted further east toward N University Blvd. There were numerous large branches downed with a few uprooted softwood trees along the path. Two homes had trees or large branches on their roofs.

An EF-0 tornado touched down south of Wardline Rd and quickly moved southeast. Multiple eyewitnesses reported seeing a tornado cross Crapanzano Road. This was corroborated by patchy tree damage visible on high resolution satellite imagery from May and June 2020 that continued southeast to I-55 and US Hwy 190. While radar imagery was disorganized along this line of storms, there was some indication that a tornado debris signature was briefly evident as the tornado crossed US Hwy 190 and moved into the neighborhoods off Old Baton Rouge Hwy. Trees were visibly damaged on between Timberlane Drive and Lato Lane, but no additional damage was identified. While local media and an emergency manager had reported power lines down and light structural damage initially, none of this could be corroborated by high resolution satellite imagery in this later analysis.

An EF-1 tornado touched down south of Brewster Road in Saint Tammany Parish and slowly tracked northwest in association with a supercell thunderstorm. The tornado produced patchy tree damage visible on high resolution satellite imagery with the most notable tree displacement and evident uproots occurring north of Brewster Road near a electrical transmission line corridor. This was collocated with a brief tornado debris signature on KLIX radar imagery during the time the tornado crossed this area. The TDS weakened thereafter and very limited patchy tree damage was visible on high resolution satellite imagery indicating the tornado weakened thereafter and dissipated before reaching Hwy 1085.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated peak winds of 110 mph was identified via high resolution satellite imagery between Greenwell Springs Rd and the Amite River. The tornado quickly intensified producing a 50 yard-wide swath of snapped and uprooted trees, predominantly pine, over inaccessible areas. The tornado crossed a small pond where it crossed a few structures, but not clearly significant damage could be identified. The tree damage continued northeast largely rural areas near the Amite River. Additional uprooted trees were identified along the river bank and east of LA 37 in Baywood, LA before the tornado damage path became less apparent. This is the estimated end point for this tornado.

An NWS Survey Team identified the damage path of an EF-1 tornado with estimated peak winds near 90 mph near Greenwell Springs, LA in East Baton Rouge Parish. This tornado touched near Denham Road causing minor tree damage in the Pinewood Acres neighborhood. As the tornado continued northeast it uprooted trees near Yatasi Avenue. It then progressed in a northeast direction where it damaged the carport and fascia of some nearby houses.  No additional damage could be identified beyond Liberty Road and it's estimated this is when the tornado lifted.

A National Weather Service Damage Assessment Team has surveyed the storm damage in Gulfport, MS. It has been determined the damage was the result of a tornado. The tornado has been rated an EF-0 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale. Damage estimates were consistent with winds of 80 mph.

Several large hardwood tree limbs snapped and minor roof damage to several homes from US Highway 90 near the aquarium to the CSX railway near Henderson Avenue. Additional tree damage was identified via high resolution satellite imagery as the tornado continued east to Courthouse Rd before it evidently dissipated.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated wind speeds as high as 95 mph touched down south of MS Hwy 67 in Harrison County, near Krohn Lane. Numerous large hardwood trees were uprooted and others had large branches broken near the start of this tornado damage path, but structural damage was minimal. The tornado continued northeast crossing MS Hwy 67 and into a new residential development area where trees were visibly damaged on high resolution satellite imagery from July 3, 2020. Additional minor tree damage was identified on high resolution satellite imagery near Tuxachanie Creek. Another swath of EF-1 damage was identified along Old Hwy 15 where individual pine trees were snapped, predominantly high snaps were observed, as well as partial roof removal or uplift on a few homes within the path. Additional minor tree damage continued northeast in collocation with a weak tornado debris signature on KMOB, and the tornado end point is estimated off the last visible point of damage on satellite imagery.

An EF-0 tornado touched down in the rural far northwest corner of Jackson County, MS where it caused minor tree damage over a narrow 25 yard wide swath. The tornado continued northeast into George County where a tornado debris signature became more evident on KMOB as it continued to produce tree damage predominantly to tree tops within dense forest, inaccessible to ground survey teams. The tornado reached its peak intensity of EF-1 south of Vestry Road where it produced a larger 50-yard-wide swath of snapped trees/ The tornado then quickly weakened as it crossed Vestry and Rebel Roads and dissipated before reaching Red Creek.

An EF-0 tornado touched down northeast of the Black Creek Cooling Pond near Big Point Road where it produced minor tree damage evident on high resolution satellite imagery. Additional tree displacement via uproots appear on high resolution satellite imagery near Grafe Homestead which was estimated to be caused by maximum sustained winds of 75 mph. This weak tornado continued to produce intermittent minor tree damage farther northeast near Cemetary and T.C. Whittington Roads in Big Point, MS. An emergency manager ground surveyed several snapped tree limbs and a pool liner lifted and carried about 40 yards near Goff Farm Road. Fortunately, no structural damage was identified from this tornado, and it's estimated that the tornado lifted shortly thereafter as no tree damage could be identified in the inaccessible forested areas to the northeast via high resolution satellite imagery.

An EF-1 tornado touched south of Percy Quin State Park Reservoir where it quickly strengthened and produced a 50-yard swath of tree damage. Numerous uprooted and snapped trees were noted in both ground survey and high resolution satellite imagery as the tornado moved northeast and crossed Mount Vernon Road and MS Hwy 48. A narrow swath of tree damage evident on high resolution satellite imagery continued across Interstate 55 before the tornado caused minor structural damage to a couple hangars at the McComb Airport. An additional uprooted tree was noted beyond this point and although a tornado debris signature remained evident on KLIX after this point, it appears the tornado weakened and lifted beyond this point and this is reflected by the weakening signature thereafter.

A sporadic damage path of minor tree damage associated with an EF-0 tornado that occurred near MS Hwy 15 in northeast Harrison County on October 28, 2020 was identified using high resolution satellite imagery. This was corroborated by collocation with a well-defined tornado vortex signature on both KLIX and KMOB radars as well as a trained weather spotter report of small limbs and tree debris as well as power flashes as this tornado crossed the highway.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum winds of 110 mph quickly developed and tracked across the Pearl River basin between Hickory, LA and Nicholson, MS. This tornado started near the Pearl River where it quickly grew in size and strength. The tornado peaked in strength causing a 200-yard-wide swath of deforestation visible on high resolution satellite imagery which was largely due to uproots and snaps. This damage path narrowed and weakened as it raced off to the northeast. Additional patches of high-end EF-0 and low-end EF-1 tree damage were identified over the Pearl River Basin as the tornado crossed into Mississippi. Additional EF-0 tree damage was identified on high resolution satellite imagery near Jackson Landing Road which was collocated with the tornado debris signature apparent on KLIX radar. The tornado appears to have lifted shortly after this point as the radar signature weakens and no further damage was identified.

An EF-1 tornado began between US Hwy 11 and Interstate 59 in Pearl River County, MS. The tornado crossed Interstate 59 with one snapped softwood pine observed in the median of the interstate. It then continued southeast and continued to strengthen before crossing McNeil Steephollow Road. At that location, there were several snapped hardwood and softwood trees, a few uprooted, but no structural damage to homes. More damage was noted just to the south at the end of Elmer Lee Road, where a chicken coop was damaged along with more softwood tree damage. The tornado continued southeast toward Bouie Road. A softwood pine was observed completely stripped of branches with the top twisted off, giving the tornado it's likely peak maximum strength of 105 mph. A few other softwoods were snapped and uprooted in the vicinity. Minor structural damage was observed nearby to a home with a portion of the roof either damaged or missing. The survey continued southeast to Anner Road, but it appeared the storm lifted between Anner and Bouie Road.

An EF-0 tornado with estimated maximum sustained winds of 80 mph touched down near MS Hwy 614 and swiftly moved southeast over inaccessible areas of Jackson County, southwest of Hurley, MS. High resolution satellite imagery from April 21, 2020 identified a clear swath of minor tree damage over the Black Creek swap which spanned as wide as 150 yards in one place. There was a brief tornado debris signature from the KMOB radar which was collocated with this tornado damage path which increased confidence in its existence.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum sustained winds of 95 mph was identified via high resolution which concurs with a prior NWS ground survey performed in April 2019. This tornado damage path aligns with a tornado vortex signature from KLIX radar with the strongest damage being uproots of large hardwood trees and partial roof removal of a manufactured home on LA Hwy 441.

An EF-2 tornado damage path that was surveyed by NWS Lake Charles in St. Landry Parish continued across the Atchafalaya River into Pointe Coupee Parish, though in a less intense state. EF-0 tree damage was identified in a narrow damage path across the river on high res satellite imagery.

A defined, narrow damage path was identified in the marsh grass near the mouth of the Jourdan River. This damage path was visible after an associated tornado vortex signature visible on KLIX radar passed over this area on January 11, 2020. No apparent damage indicators could be identified using high resolution satellite imagery.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum sustained winds of 95 mph touched down near the intersection of W.D. Walker and E Odell Walker Roads in Tangipahoa Parish. This tornado caused a 50-yard-wide swath of uprooted trees which continued east-northeast to LA Hwy 450 in Washington Parish. Pictures of this tree damage were relayed to NWS from a local emergency manager which was corroborated by high resolution satellite analysis that occurred years later. Amendments were made to the start and end point of this tornado using satellite analysis which better match up with radar presentation and existence of a tornado debris signature on KLIX radar imagery.

An EF-1 tornado touched down just west of Buford Creel Road and moved east-northeast, crossing T C Brumfield Road and James Creel Road. A home on Buford Creek Road had much of its roof removed. A nearby mobile home was destroyed and a pickup truck rolled over. Several houses on James Creel Road had considerable roof damage and a large storage building destroyed. Several large trees were also uprooted. Additional tree damage was later identified using high resolution satellite imagery to extend the of this tornado damage path across J Warren Road which coincided with the presence of a tornado debris signature on KLIX radar imagery.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated maximum sustained winds of 105 mph touched down just south of Louis Gaulden Road in Wilkinson County, MS. The tornado uprooted several trees as it headed just west of due North, becoming larger and stronger. The most significant damage occurred near Louis Gaulden and Gaulden Clinton Roads where numerous trees were snapped or uprooted, power poles were damaged, and a mobile home was rolled off its foundation and wrapped around a tree. The tornado peaked in width around 175 yards at this location. The size and strength gradually decreased as it continued northward causing mainly tree damage before lifting near Morris Smith Lane.

An EF-0 tornado with maximum sustained winds estimated near 85 mph touched down near LA Hwy 937 and Split Log Rd. The weak tornado strengthened slightly uprooting a few trees and causing minor exterior damage to a home. The tornado apparently lifted as it crossed the Saveiro Canal.

An EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds estimated near 110 mph was identified by NWS ground survey team and later via high resolution satellite analysis. The beginning of the tornado damage path was identified in minor tree damage south of Dykes Crossing, MS in Pike County. The tornado damage path remained patchy with minor tree damage and uproots noted on high resolution satellite imagery up through Silver Drive and Ginn Rd. The tornado continued northeast crossing New River Road where a large number of trees were snapped or uprooted. The tornado weakened crossing State Highway 48 before lifting near Mesa Road. Several smaller individual paths were observed in the track, indicating this was likely a multi-vortex tornado.

A NWS storm survey found that an EF-1 tornado with maximum estimated winds of 100 mph touched down near the Pascagoula River. High resolution satellite imagery helped to identify a tornado damage path in the wetland areas of the Pascagoula River basin. The tornado continued northeast damaging three camps. The most extensive damage was roof damage greater than 30 percent. A back wall was moved 3 inches due to the force of the wind, but remained structurally intact. The tornado moved northeast to Windrow Place and Prescott Road where it briefly lifted the roof off of a home, caused roof and siding damage and blew out windows. Tree damage from this tornado was also found along Foxshire Drive and Sheffield Road. The tornado apparently lifted before reaching Wilson Springs Rd, and no further damage was identified. This matched the KMOB tornado debris signature weakening beyond this point.

An EF-0 tornado damage path was identified over inaccessible areas of Jackson County, MS using high resolution satellite imagery. The tornado damage path became better defined as the tornado restrengthened east of Hwy 63 which was evident on high resolution satellite imagery. This defined damage path continued northeast with a tornado debris signature becoming apparent on KMOB radar imagery before weakening and dispersing as the tornado lifted near MS Hwy 613.

A tornadic waterspout of unknown intensity was confirmed via video by bystanders looking west from Delacroix. Exact track and time of this tornado was estimated by KLIX radar imagery of a tight tornado vortex signature that tracked between Grand Lake and Lake Lery. The tornadic waterspout was on the ground for at least a few minutes per eyewitness videos.

An EF-0 tornado touched down near the shore of Lake Pontchartrain, south of Pontchartrain Drive in Lacombe, LA. A narrow, defined path of damage to marsh grass was noted on high resolution satellite imagery that tracked northeast where increasingly more notable tree damage was visible. The most notable tree damage was several softwood trees displaced/uprooted off Pichon Road rated at around 85 mph. A tornado debris signature appeared on KLIX radar in the next frame after this point. However, the tree damage became more sparse and less notable as the tornado crossed US Hwy 190. The end point of the tornado is estimated by minor tree damage noted on high resolution satellite imagery near the conclusion of the tornado debris signature.

An EF-0 tornado touched down east of Interstate 55 in the Manchac Wildlife Management Area. A tornado debris signature was evident on KLIX radar imagery and collocated with minor marsh vegetation damage which began as a narrow (15-yard-wide), defined path visible on high resolution satellite imagery. This tornado tracked north-northeast and grew in size up to 100 yards in width after moved into Jones Island, staying over inaccessible marsh areas. The damage path evidently shrank and weakened as the tornado crossed the North Pass Manchac, and the end point was estimated by the location of the last visible vegetation damage on satellite which occurred after the dissipation of the tornado debris signature. Max estimated winds of 80 mph were estimated by evident small tree damage that occurred within the minor marsh vegetation damage.

An EF-0 tornado touched down near Sam McNeil Road in Gloster, MS causing minor tree damage as it tracked eastward. High resolution satellite imagery identified a sporadic path of uprooted/displaced trees that crossed MS Hwy 33 into rural land which coincided with a prior storm report of trees on homes in Gloster by a local EM. Maximum sustained winds were estimated around 80 mph.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated max sustained winds of 95 mph developed near MS Hwy 569 in Amite County, MS and tracked generally east-northeast over rural areas. The tornado produced minor tree damage initially before intensifying and causing intermittent swaths of snaps and uproots near Clark and Newman Roads evident on high resolution satellite imagery. The tornado damage path was estimated to have ended near Gordon Creek where tree damage became more sporadic and disorganized.

A NWS storm survey found damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado in McComb, MS. The tornado touched down around Caston Rd where minor tree damage was later identified on high resolution satellite imagery. Uprooted and snapped trees were observed near Delaware Avenue which continued northeast to Veteran Blvd. Additional tree damage was identified  along Seminole Drive to the southeast and impacted Arrowhead Drive. Several of these trees were found in a crossed direction that indicated potential rotation. These characteristics with other patches of tornadic damage nearby supported the idea of a multivortex tornado. The tornado lifted after crossing Interstate 55. The estimated peak wind speed was 100 mph.

A weak EF-0 tornado damage path was identified on high resolution satellite imagery near the Wolf River and Cemetery Rd in Harrison County, MS. Only minor defoliation and damage to tree tops was noted on satellite imagery with a more noticeable minor patch of tree damage near the bank of the Wolf River. This damage path is collocated with a tornado debris signature that was evident on KLIX radar imagery from May 11, 2019.

An EF-0 tornado damage path was identified in rural Harrison County, MS near McHenry Road. The tornado damage path consisted predominantly of minor softwood tree damage visible on high resolution satellite imagery. Maximum sustained winds were estimated near 80 mph based on high snaps of tree tops south of McHenry Rd.

Minor, EF-0 tree damage was identified near the Atchafalaya River near LA 973 indicating a continuation of this tornado damage path into Pointe Coupee Parish. The tornado continued to weaken thereafter, and the end point is estimated based on the last visible damage from high resolution satellite imagery.

A NWS storm survey found damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado and maximum sustained winds estimated near 105 mph. The storm touched down just south of Causey Road along Woodland Avenue in Prairieville, where a few to numerous trees were damaged/uprooted. The tornado continued northeast, crossing Causey Road before impacting homes along Galvez Avenue, with large hardwood branches being snapped or broken. As the tornado continued northeast, it crossed several neighborhoods including Malbrough Lane and Agnes Segua Road, with large hardwood tree bases snapped. The tornado gained its peak strength as it crossed Beech Street and Shirleyville Road. Hardwood trees had fallen and produced heavy damage to homes in the community. The tornado later caused more damage along Chris Road before crossing Louisiana Highway 933 and dissipating.

An EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds estimated near 110 mph touched down near Colyell Creek in Livingston Parish, west of LA Hwy 42. The tornado quickly strengthened causing increasingly widespread, extensive tree damage along Colyell Creek. The track reached its widest at 200 yards and most intense southwest of Larry Watts Rd and continued into Colyell, LA before gradually beginning to weaken. Most noticeable damage was mainly snapped or uprooted hardwood trees, with some power lines down as well. The tornado continued to the northeast, crossing near the intersection of Hood Road and South Satsuma Road. Flooded roads caused areas farther north along South Satsuma Road to be inaccessible for the survey. However, high resolution satellite analysis later confirmed the continuation of the weakening tornado damage path with patches of minor tree damage evident up through Perrilloux Rd before the tornado finally lifted.

A NWS storm survey found damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado near Union in St. James Parish. The tornado touched down on the east bank of the Mississippi River near the intersection of Methodist Street and Louisiana Highway 44. The storm continued northeastward, tearing the roof off of a farm utility shed. It then snapped a few more trees as it progressed northeast and lifted before it reached the Canadian National Railroad tracks. Additional minor tree damage was noted on high resolution satellite imagery to the northeast collocated with a tornado debris signature apparent on KLIX radar imagery before the tornado appeared to have lifted.

An EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds near 100 mph touched down over rural marsh areas of Livingston Parish. This tornado grew in size and strength quickly, causing a swath of numerous uprooted and damaged trees visible on high resolution satellite imagery. The damage path was widest at around 200 yards near Bayou Chene Blanc before gradually weakening and shrinking as it tracked northeast. A tornado debris signature was evident on KLIX radar imagery during this time which remained present until the tornado dissipated as it crossed Black Lake Club Rd.

A NWS storm survey found damage consistent with an EF2 tornado near Sorrento, near the intersection of Highway 10 and Highway 61. The tornado touched down at a chemical plant and lifted a single wide office trailer, snapping the buried metal tie downs. The trailer was moved 50 feet away, where it landed on several parked vehicles. The roof and 3 walls of the trailer were removed. Two employees and three contractors that were inside the trailer when the tornado struck were injured. Officials at the plant stated that the trailer was installed in February of this year. The tornado continued northeast for 1.5 miles, snapping and uprooting at least 10 power poles before it lifted near New River. Estimated peak wind 115 mph, path length 2.62 miles, path width 50 yards. Start and end points and times of this tornado were altered after high resolution satellite analysis.

An EF-1 tornado with max sustained winds estimated near 95 mph touched down just south of LA Highway 1 on St Charles Bypass Road causing minor tree damage later identified on high resolution satellite imagery. The tornado continued northeast to Reith Lane where it caused major roof damage to three well constructed, wood storage buildings and one carport. Numerous hardwood trees had large branches (8 to 22" in diameter) snapped. Tornado crossed LA1, Bayou Lafourche, Highway 308 and shifted multi-ton metal container several inches on north side of Hwy 308. The tornado continued northeast over inaccessible areas of Lafourhce Parish where minor tree damage was later identified on high resolution satellite imagery. This was collocated with a weak tornado debris signature evident on KLIX radar imagery. The end point of the tornado is estimated based on satellite imagery and the tornado debris signature dissipated after this point.

An EF-0 tornado touched down near the coast of Lake Pontchartrain in Tangipahoa Parish where it caused a defined 100-yard-wide path of minor vegetation damage visible on high resolution satellite imagery. A weak, intermittent tornado debris signature was evident on KLIX radar imagery as this tornado moved inland between Miltons Island and the Tangipahoa River. Maximum sustained winds estimated near 80 mph were based on the small trees damaged within the marsh vegetation.

An EF-1 tornado with estimated max sustained winds near 100 mph touched down on Bancroft Drive near Bayou St. John, in the vicinity of Prentiss Avenue. One house had significant damage with a large part of the roof pulled off. Several other houses had minor damage, primarily from wind blown debris. A couple of trees were snapped. Damage was classified based on damage photos and report from emergency management, as well as videos and photos of the tornado.

An EF-0 tornado was identified over inaccessible marsh areas along the southern shore of Lake Maurepas using high resolution satellite imagery from June 9, 2019. Minor marsh vegetation damage was identified along a linear path which was around 50 to 75 yards at its widest and was collocated with a tornado vortex signature on KLIX radar. Max winds were estimated near 75 mph due to minor damage to trees evident on satellite imagery.

A strong tornadic waterspout evident KLIX radar imagery developed over Lake Maurepas and tracked northeast near the shore before coming ashore briefly southwest of Galva, LA. An EF-0 tornado damage path was identified in the inaccessible marsh areas of the eastern shore of Lake Maurepas. The tornado then moved back into Lake Maurepas before coming back onto marsh areas west of Manchac and Akers, LA. A faint, defined damage path within marsh vegetation was evident on high resolution satellite imagery from June 9, 2019. The damage path weakens and becomes more obscure after the tornado crossed Anderson Canal. The tornado likely dissipated near this point as KLIX radar imagery indicated the tornado signature had weakened and occluded.

A NWS storm survey found damage consistent with a 105 mph EF1 tornado south of Killian, near Mitchell Street. The beginning of the tornado track was identified via high resolution satellite imagery where patches of minor tree damage were evident near Davidson Rd. A small outbuilding was tossed northwestward which support the existence of this tornado track. The tornado then continued north and then northwest across Lower Rome Rd and Killian Oaks Drive where a few tree tops were damaged and trees were displaced/uprooted. Near Mitchell Street, the tornado snapped and uprooted numerous trees. The trees landed in crossed directions indicating rotation, and some pointed westward indicative of the west-northwest turn the tornado took as it strengthened. The tornado progressed west, uprooting a tree in a cemetery, and then lifted after it crossed Austin Street.

The track of an EF-1 tornado with estimated winds of 105 mph was identified via high resolution satellite imagery from December 18, 2019 and January 2020. A defined damage path at 75 yards at maximum began near County Line Rd in Amite County, MS where it caused increasing damage to trees and apparently damaged a trailer or manufactured home. Near Huckleberry Lane, a swath of trees were heavily damaged/snapped on high res satellite imagery. This tree damage continued through US Hwy 98 but became less pronounced. The tornado continued east where it damaged a few outbuildings/awnings from a farm before displacing more trees along the Tangipahoa River. Tree damage became less extensive as the track reached Kenna Road and it's estimated the tornado lifted after this point.

An EF-0 tornado damage path was identified near Brookside, MS using high resolution satellite imagery from December 19, 2019. The weak tornado produced minor tree damage along a coherent, linear path near the West Fork Amite River and continued northeast where a few uprooted trees were noted on high resolution satellite imagery. The tornado apparently crossed Rollinson Rd and a few more isolated spots of minor tree damage before lifting between Fenn Rd and Townsend Rd. The beginning of the tornado damage path was harder to decipher given the close proximity to the Smithdale EF-3 tornado damage path.

NWS Storm Survey found damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado. Maximum winds 100 mph. Maximum width 75 yards. High resolution satellite imagery was used to identify the start of the tornado damage path where minor, patchy tree damage occurred near Olon Bankston Rd and Walker Rd in Tangipahoa Parish. As the tornado continued northeast, the path of tree damage became more consistent and coherent as it crossed Lofton Rd and Charles McDaniel Rd. A defined swath of tree damage occurred south of LA Hwy 440 and continued along the highway up to LA Hwy 450. Numerous trees were uprooted and a handful of trees were snapped which is the basis for the rating of this tornado. The tornado crossed Highway 450 in Washington Parish before weakening and lifting over a rural field west of Charlie Conerly Rd. This is concurrent with the weakening of radar presentation and tornado debris signature present on KLIX imagery.

A narrow, defined damage path of an EF-0 tornado was identified in the marsh vegetation near the Cataouatche Levee Canals in Jefferson Parish, LA using high resolution satellite imagery. This defined tornado damage path continued northeast, crossing Bayou Segnette and causing minor damage to trees. The damage path became less coherent as it continued into more forested areas, and it's estimated that the tornado dissipated prior to reaching Lapalco Blvd.

An EF-1 tornado touched down in the community of St. Martin, just south of Lemoyne Boulevard, and tracked northeast on an intermittent path ending near the intersection of Interstate 10 and Mississippi Highway 609. The most significant damage occurred at a strip shopping center. Plate glass windows were blown out and a portion of the roof was removed along with a portion of the wall. Several cars were overturned and rolled into the parking lot. Maximum winds were estimated at 105 mph with a path width of 75 yards.

An EF-1 tornado touched down on the northwest side of the Hammond Northshore Regional Airport just before 4am CDT where patches of large branches were snapped. The tornado continued to the north-northwest across highways 443 and 1064 where it maintained EF-0 strength by snapping small branches. The tornado remained over rural parts of northern Hammond while steadily picking up strength. The tornado was nearing peak intensity as it approached the intersection of Faller Rd and Tycer Ln at 4:06am where a house lost an awning and parts of its roof. The tornado finally reach peak intensity at as it continued to the north-northwest and approaches the intersection of Faller Rd and McManus Dr. Here, a hardwood tree was snapped in the middle. The tornado maintained this peak intensity as it approached Highway 442 where a few more hardwood trees were snapped. The tornado began weakening after this point as is continued to make its way to the north-northwest and across the Tangipahoa River. As it crossed the river, it practically paralleled S Bennett Rd in Independence, LA where it mainly snapped large branches until it lifted shortly before Highway 40 at 4:14am.

An EF-1 touched down south of Robert, LA at 5:11am CDT along Traino Rd where a few small limbs were snapped. The tornado quickly gained strength as it moved north towards Highway 22. The tornado reached peak intensity as a 90mph EF-1 at 5:14am along Highway 20 just east of the Fairhope Subdivision where a group of softwood trees were snapped. This tornado maintained peak intensity as it continued northward towards Crown Dr at 5:18am. Here more softwood trees were snapped and a roof was partially removed from a manufactured home. The tornado began to weaken and occlude as it moved to the northeast. The tornado broke small limbs and caused small trees to lean in a convergent pattern before lifting at 5:21am just southwest of Jasper Ln south of Robert, LA.

A brief EF-0 touched down just north of Highway 445 on Bailey Loop Rd in Loranger, LA at 4:57am CDT where it broke off patches of large branches. This tornado continued due northward into forested areas of Loranger before making it to Bradford Ln at 5:04am. Here, more patches of small and large branches were broken before the tornado lifted along Highway 40 still at 5:04am.

Minor vegetation damage was identified using high resolution satellite imagery to verify the track of a radar-confirmed EF-0 tornado that occurred near Blind River, southeast of Denson, LA in Livingston Parish. This weak tornado remained away from any structures though a weak, but conclusive tornado debris signature was visible on KHDC radar imagery which aligned with intermittent minor vegetation over the marsh. Most notably a few trees were visibly displaced on the bank of Blind River.

The tornado touched down near Arlington Elementary School and moved northeastward causing generally minor damage to trees and a few residential roofs. The tornado was strongest as it approached Old Mobile Ave, where it uprooted several trees in a convergent pattern on the south side of the road near the intersection with Chateau Dr. As the tornado approached the north side of the Briarwood subdivision along Arrowhead Dr, it began to occlude, taking turn to the north and then northwest as it approached Industrial Ave. It then crossed Hwy 90 and lifted as it approached Shortcut Rd.

An EF-0 tornado crossed Bear Island Road south of Hwy 22, causing damage mostly to trees. One home suffered minor roof damage, and a large metal canopy was thrown about 25 to 40 yard to the southeast. High resolution satellite imagery helped to identify the start and end of this tornado over the inaccessible marsh areas where defoliation and minor tree damage were evident. The marsh damage was also collocated with an evident tornado debris signature on KHDC radar imagery.

An EF-1 tornado was confirmed by NWS ground survey northeast of Hammond, LA in Tangipahoa Parish. The ground survey first noted damage near River Rd and Arbuthnott Drive. The tornado moved generlaly northward to the east of River Rd causing additional damage mostly to trees. The strongest damage surveyed was along Dummyline Rd where it snapped several large trees, before the tornado crossed the Tangipahoa River. It then caused additional tree damage and destroyed a tent on the grounds of Camp Living Waters. Remote survey techniques utilizing high-resolution satellite imagery identified additional damage just north of Dusty Ln between Diana Ln and Calmes Ln, as a start point. The same imagery also indicated the tornado continued past the cap ground, causing tree damage near Robert Perkins Rd, and gradually weakening and lifting as it approached Chappepeela Rd.

The damage path of an EF-1 tornado was identified via high resolution satellite imagery over inaccessible rural marsh areas of Terrebonne Parish, southwest of Humphreys, LA. This tornado began southwest of Blue Bird Canal where a defined, but weak path of damage to marsh vegetation was evident. The marsh damage continued northeast across Blue Bird Canal, narrowly missing fishing camps, and was collocated with a strong tornado vortex signature on KHDC radar imagery. The tornado intensified as it crossed the Intracoastal Waterway and Lake Hackberry with numerous displaced trees evident along a canal and maximum path width around 250 yards. This was collocated with a tornado debris signature that was identified on Climavision V014 radar imagery. The tornado continued northeast and quickly weakened which is corroborated by KHDC and Climavision radar imagery. The tornado evidently lifted prior to reaching structures along Bayou Black Drive in Humphreys.

An apparent waterspout came onshore near Market Street, producing light to moderate damage for several blocks inland before dissipating. Damage included several downed trees and downed fencing. Two homes were damaged by falling trees. Maximum estimated wind speed 85 mph, damage path 0.25 miles and path width 200 yards.

Two homes were damaged by falling trees. Maximum estimated wind speed 85 mph, damage path 0.25 miles and path width 200 yards.

Two homes were damaged by falling trees. Maximum estimated wind speed 85 mph, damage path 0.25 miles and path width 200 yards.
