An EF-1 tornado was confirmed by NWS ground survey teams in Gautier, MS. The tornado touched down east of Ladnier Rd near Bacot Park where it stripped flashing off of apartments and broke large tree limbs as it moved northward. It caused minor damage on Connie Circle where it displaced a tree. This early damage, while intermittent aligns with a weak tornado debris signature in KMOB radar data. The tornado then began to intensify and reached an initial peak of EF-1 strength just south of US Hwy 90 causing substantial damage to a restaurant and a concentrated area of tree damage. The tornado moved northward across US Hwy 90 causing tree damage on the campus of the Mississippi Gulf Coast Community College. It weakened to EF-0 intensity and continued to cause minor damage to homes and trees as it moved from the Northwood Hills community through Westgate Parkway. The tornado continued to move northeastward and restrengthened as it approached Martin Bluff Road. In this area, it caused roof damage to several buildings in the Bayou Village Apartments complex. In the Glenmark Apartments complex, the tornado destroyed a large wood-frame shed, shifted a small outbuilding from its foundation, and caused roof damage to several buildings. It also caused roof damage and the collapse of the parapet wall facade on two buildings at the Riverbend Condominiums in the same area. The tornado crossed Martin Bluff Road causing increasingly isolated tree and roof damage across Victoria Dr and Courtney St. The ground survey was unable to access areas north of Courtney St. However, high-resolution satellite imagery indicates the tornado caused a narrow swath of tree damage in the forested area between Courtney St. and I-10. A Mississippi Department of Transportation traffic camera showed the tornado become disorganized with only intermittent ground circulations as it crossed Interstate 10. The tornado damage path was identified via high-resolution satellite analysis east of the Hickory Hills neighborhood in Jackson County, MS. As the tornado reorganized it strengthened once more causing evident vegetation scarring in the marsh and damage to trees visible on the high-resolution satellite imagery. The damage path peaked around 125 yards in width. The tornado continued northeast where a tornado debris signature became evident in KMOB radar data again. The debris signature continued as the tornado gradually weakened over the river basin. The tornado is estimated to have lifted over the Pascagoula River Basin near Clark Bayou and west of Hwy 63.

A EF-1 tornado struck western Marion County near Kokomo. The tornado caused some tree damage near the intersection of Welch Lane and Branton Bay Road. The tornado tracked northeast closely following Branton Bay Road up to Highway 98. The tornado did cause some roof damage to Popwell's Family Restaurant on Hwy 98. The tornado ended as it crossed Old Hwy 24. Max winds estimated at 90 MPH.

An EF1 tornado touched down just southwest of Fordsville Road east of Tylertown, MS. The worst damage damage was seen just northeast of the touchdown point with light damage to a roof and an area of tree damage with multiple uprooted trees. Light tree damage was noted along the path until it crossed into Marion County where the roof of a restaurant was damaged on Highway 98.

This tornado began near the Pike Lincoln County line and tracked northeastward across southern Lincoln County, snapping and uprooted numerous trees along its path. The broad damage footprint with embedded areas of more concentrated damage suggests this path consisted of multiple vortices originating from the same parent mesocyclone. Tree damage was most prolific west of I-55 along Jack Lane, Summit Drive, Beachnut Trail, Wallace Drive, Montgomery Road, Dry Creek Trail, and Homesville Road. A house on Jack Lane sustained shingle damage, and outbuildings were damaged on Montgomery Road, Beachnut Trail, and Big Creek Drive. A mobile home was also damage along Beachnut. The tornado crossed I-55 about two miles south of the Bogue Chitto exit, then weakened and lifted as it moved through the Norfield community. This tornado may have began farther southwest, and additional surveys are planned to determine the correct starting point.

This tornado began near Mount Olive Road and tracked northeastward across Shell Oil Lane and MS Highway 583 before lifting near Highway 583 and Bethel Road. Along the tornado path, the roof of an outbuilding was damage on Shell Oil Lane and a house on Highway 583 sustained roof damage. Several trees were snapped and uprooted.

An EF-1 tornado touched down along August Ln, just west of Polo Rd, where it damaged trees and a roof of a residence. It moved east along Polo Rd with sporadic tree damage. The heaviest tree damage occurred along Spirit of the Forest Ln and River Trace Rd where more than a dozen very large trees were uprooted or snapped. Periodic tree and large limb damage occurred along the remainder of the path until the tornado lifted in the Merrywood Estates subdivision. Path length was 9.3 miles. Max width was 75 yards. Estimated maximum winds around 95 mph.

An EF-1 tornado touched down along Ancil Cox Road on the southwest
side of Centreville near Griffin Lane. A few trees were downed
here, with at least one of them taking down a powerline. The
tornado tracked northeast, generally paralleling Ancil Cox Road until reaching the intersection with Old Highway 33. The most significant damage occurred near this intersection, where approximately a dozen trees were uprooted or snapped which
supports the 90 mph wind rating. Beyond this point, the tornado
continued northeastward producing sporadic tree and limb damage
along its path. The final accessible point of the tornado’s track was near Centerville Academy. Minor damage was observed on campus, including a downed light pole, partial roof uplift on a metal building, and a baseball pitching backstop that was thrown
approximately 250 feet into a chain-link fence. The tornado continued northeast beyond Lower Centreville Rd where additional minor tree damage was identified. The tornado evidently lifted over inaccessible areas to the survey team prior to reaching Hwy 48.

An EF-0 tornado with maximum sustained winds estimated near 80 mph touched down near US Hwy 98 in far northeast Amite County, MS causing minor tree damage and removing a few metal panels from an outbuilding. The tornado continued northeast into Pike County where additional minor tree damage was found on Kenna Rd. This tornado continued northeast into Lincoln County, MS where it strengthened into a large, strong EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds near 110 mph southwest of Norfield, MS in NWS Jackson's area.

An NWS Storm Survey team determined that an EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 105 mph embedded in a line of storms touched down just north of the Ascension/Assumption Parish line shortly after midnight on February 15th. This tornado started by snapping large branches and uprooting a tree on Te Nom Landry Rd and Highway 308 south. It continued moving east-northeast over open fields until reaching the intersection of Highway 3089 and Highway 70. Here, the tornado strengthened to EF-1 intensity where a power pole and tree trunk were snapped. This tornado maintained intensity as it continued moving to the east-northeast where it snapped a series of powerlines off of Baxter Rd on the south side of the CF Industries complex. The tornado then started to intensify to peak intensity as it crossed into St. James Parish while moving through mainly open fields before it hit a series of powerlines in an open field, snapping 4 lines. It maintained strength as it crossed another open field along Badger Rd before snapping another series of powerlines along Highway 18. The tornado appeared to weaken as it continued to move east-northeast across the Mississippi River. When the tornado reached Highway 3125, it partially snapped/bent another powerline. The tornado then moved into the marsh north of Highway 3025 before reaching Highway 61 where it uprooted another tree. The last damage point for this tornado is along I-10 where it snapped more large branches. This track remains preliminary and additional changes could occur after further review of high-resolution satellite imagery in the coming weeks.

An NWS storm survey team determined that an EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 90 mph embedded in a line of storms touched down along Highway 1 in far western Ascension Parish just after midnight on February 15th. Here, the tornado mainly snapped large branches and small tree trunks. The tornado continued eastward causing minor tree damage as it crossed Hwy 405 and the Mississippi River. More damage to large branches and tree tops was observed along River Rd north of Darrow. The tornado continued to move east-southeast over inaccessible areas until it reached Ruffin Martinez Rd and Highway 22. More large branches were snapped here as it continued moving to the east-southeast towards Walter Hill Rd. The tornado then crossed the Mississippi River a 2nd time and remained over either open fields or a refinery. The tornado reached peak intensity as it approached Highway 70 where it snapped a few tree trunks. The tornado then continued over the marsh east of Highway 70 where it likely lifted near the Ascension/St. James Parish line. This track remains preliminary and additional changes could occur after further review of high-resolution satellite imagery in the coming weeks.

An NWS storm survey team confirmed an EF-0 with peak winds of 85 mph touched down on the southern side of Thibodaux at 12:45am on February 15th. An trailer RV was partially flipped off of Talbot Ave as the tornado moved to the east-southeast. The tornado then reach peak strength as it impacted Tabernacle Pentecostal Church. Here, the tornado lifted a significant portion of the roof of the church, displacing it to the east. The wood framing of the roofing had evidence of rotting, so the lower bound for that damage was used in this case. The tornado slightly weakened as it moved east-southeast where minimal damage was found until it reached Percy Brown Rd where multiple large branches were snapped along it. The tornado remained very weak as it continued to the east-southeast where the next notable damage came on Crossing North St where a backyard awning was uplifted. As the tornado moved to the east-northeast, vegetation damage was noted on high-resolution imagery north of Rousseau where the tornado appeared to have lifted though additional analysis will be needed to determine the end point over inaccessible areas. This track remains preliminary and additional changes could occur after further review of high-resolution satellite imagery in the coming weeks.

An NWS ground survey confirmed an EF-0 tornado with maximum sustained winds estimated near 85 mph tracked across parts of southwest and central Washington Parish, south of Franklinton, LA. This tornado began near Sam Smith Rd where it snapped a rotted large hardwood tree. Additional tree debris and minor damage to skirting of a manufactured home was noted nearby. The tornado continued east-northeast where more abundant tree damage was identified including snaps of smaller tree trunks and branches along Daniels Rd, west of LA Hwy 25. This was where the tornado appeared to be at its strongest and widest, spanning about 75 yards wide. The tornado continued across the Bogue Chitto River, and additional minor tree damage was found by the survey team along LA Hwy 16, Tullos Rd, and Gene Williams Rd. Near Lee Rd and Thigpen Rd was where the last area of more concentrated minor tree damage was noted and this was the estimated end point of this tornado damage path. No additional damage was identified on Moore Rd nor LA Hwy 439, however, minor sporadic tree damage did continue farther east, north of Bogalusa, LA.

An NWS storm survey with additional support from a local storm report has confirmed an EF-1 tornado occurred west of Liberty, MS in Amite County. This tornado began near Berwick Cassels and Smylie Rd where minor tree damage was identified. Additional patches of increasingly more notable tree damage as wide as 175 yards occurred as the tornado tracked northeast along Smylie Rd. The tornado reached peak intensity with maximum sustained winds near 100 mph as it approached Enterprise Rd and Lower Liberty Gloster Rd where it snapped tree trunks and uprooted numerous trees. A manufactured observed minor roof damage. More tree damage from this tornado was identified where it crossed MS Hwy 24, moving northeast. A local storm report identified additional notable tree damage with a few uprooted/snapped medium to large pine trees near Rattlesnake Ln. This tornado evidently lifted near the Amite River just before reaching Old 24 Compromise Rd.

An EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 100 mph tracked north of Peoria, MS in Amite County. The tornado began near Patterson Rd where minor tree damage was identified. This minor tree damage continued northeast becoming gradually more severe as the tornado approached Peoria Rd. Dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted near and northeast of Peoria Rd as the tornado reached its peak intensity and a width of 200 yards. The tornado tracked northeast, crossing Highway 24 and East Fork Rd. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted along this portion of the path. Additional minor tree damage was identified beyond East Fork Rd and Pray Rd on high resolution satellite imagery as the tornado evidently weakened. Patches of tree damage continued through Bass, Barron, Brown, and Thompson roads. Another brief swath of heavier tree damage occurred north of Thompson Rd as the tornado briefly reached a second peak at EF-1 intensity. The tornado weakened again and is estimated to have lifted after crossing Tangipahoa Rd and Hwy 570 where tree damage became too sparse to be considered a coherent track.

The track of an EF-0 tornado was identified via high resolution satellite imagery in association with a quick spin-up circulation within a line of storms. This tornado evidently damaged and/or displaced trees along mainly inaccessible areas near South Valley Rd and Kennedy Creek in Pearl River County, MS. The damage path peaked at approximately 70 yards wide and apparently ended after crossing Sun Valley Acres Rd, prior to reaching John Amacker Rd.

An EF-1 tornado touched down just west of MS Highway 13 near Mapp Turner Road and moved east-southeast across Greens Creek Road, Friendship Road, Martin Bass Road, Carson-Bunker Hill Road and Hunnicut Road before crossing MS Highway 35. It lifted near Bassfield-Cemetery Road as it merged with the Choctaw Road tornado. The tornado uprooted and snapped multiple trees along the path and downed power lines. A few trees were down on a couple buildings and homes. Some tin damage was noted to some sheds and skirting blown away from a mobile home.

An EF-1 tornado began west of Chocktaw Road, tracked east-southeast into the west side of the town of Bassfield, and dissipated shortly after crossing MS Hwy 35 as it . Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted on the south and southwest side of Chocktaw Rd. A mobile home had the roof heavily damaged. A tree was down on a vehicle, and power lines were blown down. A wooden building was damaged on the edge of a pond as well. A well defined path of snapped and uprooted trees was left from just south of Chocktaw Rd southeastward to north of Dyess Trail. Estimated peak winds were 105 mph. Max width was 250 yards. Path length was 2.22 miles.

An EF-1 tornado touched down along Carson Bunkerhill Road, moved east across Bass Burkett Road and Lofton Drive, and dissipated as it crossed MS Highway 35. A few trees were snapped, but mostly trees were uprooted along the path. A couple power lines were down as well.

An EF-1 tornado touched down just east of MS Highway 42, tracked east, and dissipated in Covington County near MS Highway 589. Trees were damaged along Hosey Mikell, Ridgeland, Stubbs, and Willie Buckley roads. A notable increase in intensity was seen as the tornado approached and crossed Gum Swamp Road around Fairman Lane. Hundreds of trees were snapped and uprooted in the area. Some minor damage was done to a few structures on the east side of Gum Swamp Rd. The tornado continued east into Covington County at Sumrall Road where additional trees were uprooted. The tornado continued northeast and ended along MS Highway 589 where it uprooted a tree and snapped some large branches.

An EF-0 tornado rapidly developed within a line of storms near Blond, LA in St. Tammany Parish. This tornado predominantly uprooted and damaged tree branches and rotten trunks as it tracked from Allison Rd east-southeast through LA Hwy 40 before dissipating near Lee Road Junior High School.

An EF-1 tornado touched down along Glade Lane and tracked east-southeast across Fox Road and through the north side of the Ruth and Bristerville communities. The tornado dissipated near the intersection of Silverton Trail and Orchard Lane, but additional straightline wind damage to trees and power lines continued south and east of the tornado path.

Another EF-0 tornado rapidly developed within a line of storms subsequently after the dissipation of an EF-0 tornado to its southwest. This tornado caused minor tree damage along a defined path from a private residence west of LA Hwy 1082 and Elderberry Ln east-southeast to LA Hwy 1083 near Oalmann Rd.

An EF-1 tornado quickly spun up along a line of strong thunderstorms near Liverpool LA, south of LA 1043. This tornado caused predominantly uprooted and snapped trees as it tracked southeast. It began to track more east-southeast as it approached I-55, bringing a few trees down along the interstate before dissipating.

A tornado rapidly developed within a strong line of thunderstorms southeast of Lewiston, LA along LA 38. This tornado predominantly caused minor tree damage as it tracked southeastward, maintaining EF-0 intensity until it reached LA Hwy 440. The tornado strengthened, uprooting trees and snapping large branches and the tops of trunks as it LA Hwy 10 and moved into Sylvest Rd. Additional tree damage was identified downstream of the surveyed end point of this tornado via high resolution satellite imagery which evidently dissipated prior to it reaching LA Hwy 16, west of Franklinton.

An EF-1 tornado quickly spun up along a line of strong thunderstorms just east of Franklinton Louisiana. The tornado tracked southeastward through southern areas of Franklinton, causing moderate roof damage to a Floral Shop and damaging a business sign. As it crossed LA 16 it caused minor roof damage to homes and an apartment building while also causing a couple trees to fall on a home and shed. After the tornado crossed south of LA 1072 it caused moderate damage to a forecast, snapping about 40-50 small pine trees. This was the peak intensity of the tornado. This tornado caused several uprooted and snapped trees along its path. Finally the tornado tracked further southeast, before dissipating east of LA 1072 north of Fussell Road.

An EF-0 tornado with maximum sustained winds of 85 mph rapidly developed along a line of thunderstorms, near Hwy 1047 and Deer Creek Rd, near the Tangipahoa and St. Helena Parish border. The crossed the parish line and into a solar farm where a weak tornado debris signature became evident on KHDC. Only minor tree damage was evident along this section of Interstate 55. Additional tree damage including uproots and snapped tree tops were evident between I-55 and Hwy 51 which was collocated with a stronger tornado debris signature on KHDC. The tornado likely peaked in strength and size in this area. Thereafter, the tornado crossed the Tangipahoa River and began moving east-southeast toward LA Hwy 10. The tornado caused mostly uprooted trees and snapped large limbs off of trees along LA Hwy 10 before it dissipated just east of LA 1054.
A 65 MPH EF0 tornado touched down north of Albany, LA and tracked southeast. The damage was large branches and tree debris along the line that also lines up with the radar signature.

A strong EF-1 tornado touched down south of Hwy 570 near Moak Rd in Pike County, MS. This tornado rapidly strengthened and grew in size as it tracked to the east-northeast causing destruction of a wood frame farm outbuilding and increasingly significant treefall as it crossed Kodiak Ln and Magnolia Pisgah Rd. While there was a high proportion of treefall associated with this tornado in this area, a significant portion was due to uprooted trees from soft soils due to recent heavy rainfall. As such, maximum sustained winds were estimated near 110 mph despite the heavy deforestation. This level of heavy deforestation spanning approximately 375 yards wide at peak width continued across Hwy 570 to Dye Ln where tree damage rapidly became less significant. It's estimated that this tornado rapidly lifted as it crossed Magee Rd.

A subsequent EF-1 tornado rapidly developed along Hwy 570, northwest of McComb, MS on the evening of March 11th. This tornado damage path was distinctly different to the predecessor tornado along Hwy 570 as most of the confluent damage was directed southeast and east as the KHDC velocity signature denoted the strong rotation center of this tornado diving southeastward along the highway. This tornado uprooted several trees, snapped the tops of tree trunks, tossed a golf cart approximately 70 yards, and caused minor roof damage to a couple homes as it tracked along the highway. Additional tree damage was near Delaware Avenue where confluence of damage direction was identified. These damage path characteristics are subject to change.

An EF-1 tornado with peak estimated winds near 105 mph touched down near Johnston Chapel Rd. A broadly defined area of uprooted and damaged trees was identified via high resolution satellite imagery near the beginning of this path which continued east-southeast across Old Brookhaven Rd and Interstate 55. This damage was corroborated by a storm report with pictures of numerous uprooted trees near Chapel Dr. The tornado began to strengthen and increase in size with complex multi-vortex behaviors evident according to the damage path present after crossing I-55. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted along US Hwy 51, Rollingwood Drive, and Johnston Station Rd. Additional tree damage was surveyed near Lakeside Drive on Southwest Mississippi Community College campus. The tornado continued east destroying a cinderblock dugout at North Pike High School. More tree damage was also identified on Barrett Drive and Jaguar Trail. Scattered pockets of wind damage were evident via ground survey across Barnett Rd and L.D. White Rd, but the most cohesive damage points representative of a tornado damage path were evident to the southeast as the multi-vortex tornado continued to produce patches and swaths of tree damage on high resolution satellite imagery near Hwy 570 and and River Ridge Rd. The tornado damage path evidently became incoherent after crossing River Ridge Rd and it's estimated the tornado lifted prior to reaching Hwy 44.

The EF-0 tornado began along Mason Ferry Rd west of Georgetown Alabama where it resulted in a few large branches snapped. The tornado continued through the forest on the north side of Mason Ferry rd before reaching McDavid rd where large branches and a large softwood tree was uprooted in a clear convergent pattern. This is likely where the tornado ended. The tornado may have reached peak intensity within the woods; however, the peak intensity that survey crews were able to find was around 85 mph.

The EF-1 tornado began south of Mason Ferry Rd just south of McDavid Rd. The start point was difficult to determine as the end point of another tornado and the start point of this tornado were close in proximity to each other. However, convergence patterns and radar analysis did suggest that these were indeed two separate tornadoes rather than one tornado. The tornado snapped a couple of softwood trunks and continued sporadically uprooting trees as it approached Brothers Lake Club Rd where it reached peak intensity of 90 mph near and around Brothers Lake Club Rd. The tornado then crosses Mason Ferry Rd and tracked into the forest before crossing Alabama 217 and finishing near Cleborne Rd.

A weak and small EF-0 tornado occurred in the woods just west of highway 63 just north of Jake Hill Rd. A tree was uprooted and a few large branches snapped off along highway 63 as it crossed the road. The tornado likely dissipated just east of highway 63.
This brief EF-0 started along Grice Road where it uprooted a couple of trees. The tornado crossed Bunker Hill Road and ended along Highway 35, where it uprooted a couple more trees.

A EF-0 tornado touched down just east of Loranger. This was determined via a ground survey point and minor tree damage evident on high resolution satellite imagery. This track was collocated with a weak tornado debris signature evident on radar imagery from nearby KHDC.

The damage path of an EF-0 tornado with estimated peak winds near 85 mph was identified on high resolution satellite imagery in northeast Wilkinson County, just south of Perrytown, MS. Evident damage and displacement to trees were identified near Turkey Creek Rd which extended to the east-southeast through to Tom Crum Rd which was closely collocated to a strong area of rotation on KHDC. The majority of this damage path was inaccessible to ground survey and thus is rated via identifiable damage via satellite.

The damage path of an EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds of 90 mph was identified via high resolution satellite imagery in northwest Amite County, north of Gloster, MS. This tornado caused damage and displacement to several trees including a swath west of Hwy 33 concurrent with an EF-1. Additional tree damage was identified along Hwy 33 near Homochitto Rd. This was corroborated by EM report of tree damage north of Gloster which also was collocated with a strong area of rotation on KHDC radar.

The damage path of an EF-0 tornado with maximum sustained winds near 85 mph was identified on high resolution satellite imagery in rural Amite County, north of Liberty, MS. Damage to tree tops were identified west of Pecan Rd. A focused path of damaged and displaced trees continued east-southeast through Meadville and Freeman Rd. The tornado evidently lifted after crossing Hwy 569, prior to reaching Newman Rd where no other damage could be identified. This damage path was collocated with a strong area of rotation of KHDC radar imagery.

An EF-2 tornado touched down north of Krotz Springs where it flipped and and destroyed a mobile home. The tornado continued along its path tipping another mobile home on its roof and destroying 2 others by flying debris and knocking them off their blocks. The tornado continued on producing minor roof damage to 6 other homes and damaging farm equipment. Four people received minor injuries in a mobile home. The max estimated wind speed was 115 mph. The tornado continued briefly into Point Coupee Parish, producing EF-0 damage in forested areas.

The damage path of an EF-0 tornado was identified in extreme southwestern East Feliciana Parish, near Lindsay, LA. This tornado caused predominantly minor tree damage including evident displacement and damage of trees consistent with maximum estimated winds near 80 mph. The tornado tracked from King's View Circle through Highway 964 and Thompson Rd before evidently dissipating over open rural fields near the parish line. This damage path was closely located with a strong area of rotation on KHDC radar imagery.

Available high resolution satellite imagery revealed a brief EF0 tornado occurred west of Lucedale just off of Country Hill Road. This tornado likely began further west with the start point being approximated based on radar data showing a tornado debris signature beginning in the vicinity of the ponds west of Country Hill Road. The tornado remained solely within forest causing minor tree damage.

Available high resolution satellite imagery revealed a brief EF0 tornado occurred solely within forest just east of FS Road 342 near Janice. The tornado caused minor tree damage to the forest for roughly a third of a mile before lifting.

Available high resolution satellite imagery revealed a brief EF0 tornado occurred just southeast of Gatlin Creek Road west of Vernal. This tornado caused just under a quarter mile of minor forest damage, crossing no accessible roads.

This tornado began near Pleasant Hill Dr and Elm Ln in southeastern Lincoln County and tracked eastward crossing MS Hwy 583 in the Ruth community. It then continued along Greenwood Rd and Raiford Trail, crossing Primitive Dr in the Bristerville community, then across Monument Trail and Junction Ln before crossing into southwestern Lawrence County  near the Verna community. It crossed Holmesville Rd and paralleled Verna Rd before lifting near Excell Boyd Rd. Along the path, countless trees were snapped or uprooted, some in large swaths, multiple outbuildings sustained damage, utility poles were damaged, a mobile home sustained roof and skirting damage, and a site built home sustained minor wind damage.

This tornado began near Junction Ln and Ard Trail in far southeastern Lincoln County, crossing Holmesville Rd as it moved into far southwestern Lawrence County. It continued across Woody Rd, WS Cothern Rd, and MS Hwy 44, passing near the center of the Jayess community. It then tracked across Jessie Wallace Rd and Latrell Sanders Rd before lifting just north of the Walthall County line. Along the path, numerous trees were snapped or uprooted, some in large swaths. Utility poles were leaning or broken with several lines downed. Multiple homes and outbuildings sustained roof and siding damage, and a business received roof damage. A poultry farm southeast of Jayess sustained roof damage.

Available high resolution satellite imagery revealed a brief EF0 tornado occurred in the Turnerville area, tracking across Dixie Road and Salco Road. The tornado caused mainly minor tree damage along its track, reaching a peak width just across Salco Road around 100 yards wide.

Available high resolution satellite imagery revealed a weak EF0 tornado tracked from Irvington through the Laurendine area causing sporadic minor tree damage all along its track. The track of the tornado is further supported by the presence of a radar tornado debris signature.

Available high resolution satellite imagery was able to reveal an EF0 tornado tracked north of Interstate 10 near Grand Bay. The tornado began north of Pascagoula Road solely in forest causing minor tree damage. While there is a gap in visible damage on satellite imagery, radar tornado debris signature persists east connecting with another small corridor of tree damage near Ramsey Road and Boe Road. This is where the end point of the tornado is estimated as any visible damage associated with the radar signature disappears after reaching I-10.

Available high resolution satellite imagery revealed a brief EF0 tornado tracked along Dawes Road just northeast of Dees. The tornado remained primarily within forest causing minor tree damage along its track. The track of the tornado is also supported by a radar tornado debris signature.

An EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 90 mph touched down at 30th Street and Brookwood Ave. where large branches were down on several trees.  Similar damage occurred as the tornado tracked to the southeast across Pass Road and 28th Street.  The tornado continued to the southeast and reached peak intensity of 90 mph near the intersection of 5th Avenue and 25th Street where a few trees were snapped.  One hardwood tree was hollow inside and the other snap was very high.  Numerous large branches were also removed from several trees near this intersection. The tornado then continued to the southeast crossing Gulf Avenue near 24th Street producing sporadic tree damage.  The tornado then crossed the railroad just west of Hewes St. where additional large branches were removed from several trees all the way to  Beach Blvd.  Numerous trees were observed to have lost large branches between Roberts Ave. and Centennial Plaza on Beach Blvd.

An EF-1 tornado with peak winds of 95 mph touched down just north of the intersection of Ohio Ave. and Polk St. in Gulfport.  The tornado quickly tracked to the south causing sporadic tree damage that consisted primarily of large tree limbs being blown off of trees as tracked southward between Wisconsin Ave. and West Railroad Ave.  
The tornado continued south-southeast across US Hwy 49 with no visible damage observed to commercial businesses in this area. Large branches and snapped trees were observed between 34th St. and 28th St. At the intersection of 28th St. and 22nd Ave. two metal buildings had their garage doors blown out as the tornado continued south-southeast. At the intersection of Pass Road and 22nd Ave. a large hardwood tree was completely uprooted. The tornado then crossed Pass Road and produced additional tree damage in the form of large branches and a few snapped trees. The snapped trees were either dead and diseased, so the windspeed was lowered to 90 mph to account for this. As the tornado crossed the railroad track, minor damage to a home in the form of a few removed shingles was observed on 3rd St. The tornado then moved offshore near the intersection of Beach Blvd. and Pratt Ave.

A NWS ground survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds of 100 mph tracked across portions of southeast Pike and southwest Walthall counties. This tornado began near Smithburg, MS at the intersection of Mount Herman Road and Silver Drive where it uprooted a tree and snapped branches. Additional EF-0 tree damage was identified on Blanchard and Smithburg roads. The tornado peaked in intensity as an EF-1 as it approached and crossed over Harold Alford and Magnolia roads uprooting and snapping several trees as well as taking down wooden transmission lines. The tornado then weakened causing EF-0 damage to trees and removed metal panels from an industrial chicken coop as it approached Brockdale Rd and then crossed the Bogue Chitto River. The end point of this tornado is estimated to be after it crossed MS Hwy 27 where it caused additional tree damage in a confluent pattern.

A NWS ground survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado with maximum sustained winds of 95 mph tracked across portions of northern Walthall County, near Enon, MS. This tornado began near L. L. Coon Rd where high resolution satellite imagery showed areas of damaged/displaced trees on a confined path that tracked southeast toward MS Hwy 44. The tornado strengthened to EF-1 strength as it moved into Willie Cothern Rd where it uprooted trees, lifted metal roofing off a porch, and snapped a swath of tree trunks on private property. The tornado turned more eastward as it crossed MS Hwy 583 where it damaged shingles on a church and uprooted and snapped a few trees. Minor tree damage continued eastward across New Home Church Road with plenty of tree debris across the road. This tree damage gradually became more sparse and less concentrated near Harvey Ratliff Road. It's possible that this tornado is continuous with the Darbun, MS EF-0 that occurred shortly after this tornado, but there is not enough supporting evidence at this time. Additional revisions to this tornado are possible after high resolution satellite analysis is conducted over the coming weeks.

A NWS ground survey confirmed an EF-0 tornado tracked across portions of northeast Walthall County, near Darbun, MS. The tornado damage path began near MS Hwy 27 and Dee Toney Rd where numerous trees were leaning in a confluent pattern with large branches down. Minor tree damage continued with uproots and large branches predominantly occurring along the damage path on Jordan, Sauls, and Dan Holmes roads. This tornado continued east-southeast into Marion County, within the NWS Jackson area of responsibility, where additional EF-0 and EF-1 tree damage was identified by ground survey. It's estimated that the tornado lifted near Circle Bar Road. Additional revisions to this tornado are possible after high resolution satellite analysis is conducted over the coming weeks.

This tornado moved into Marion County from northeastern Walthall County near the Darbun community, crossing Reid Rd, Bracey Rd, and Rowley Rd before lifting near Circle Bar Road. Along the path, multiple trees and tree limbs were downed.

A tornado touched down west of the intersection of Old Highway 61 Perry Town Rd, near Ireland, MS. This tornado does connect into Jackson's area and moves through Adams and Franklin Counties before continuing northeast. This is a long track EF3 tornado but the part in Wilkinson is rated EF2 based on large swatches of snapped trees. The majority of the damage is to trees with multiple larger sheds and double wides with damage or destroyed. It's unlikely this is where the track actually started but we are waiting for additional drone and satellite data. It's also possible the track changes as we start to discern tornado damage from straight line wind damage. This info will all be updated as new data arrives.

This tornado began near or just west of the intersections of Tatum Salt Dome Road and R Thompson Road where it produced damage to trees crossing Tatum Salt Dome Road with a narrow swath of damage produced just north of R Thompson Rd. The tornado moved eastward through the Half Moon Creek lower wooded areas before crossing Beach Road, retaining a consolidated core that could be observed transecting the roadway twice as the tornado track roughly paralleled the roadway. The tornado produced mostly vegetative damage until it approached western Purvis in the Boggy Hollow areas where outbuildings were damaged or destroyed and trees were blown onto homes. A mobile home was blown off of its blocks just north of Purvis-to-Columbia Road, which the tornado approximately moved along as it traveled east-northeast. The tornado intensified to its maximum intensity, producing damage that suggests maximum winds were at least 135 mph as several residences had roofs removed as well as some exterior walls blown inward near Coal Town Road and Highway 589. Significant damage was also observed along Central Industrial Row where several metal building system (MBS) warehouse and commercial buildings had walls and internal structures including pillars/columns and roofing purlins destroyed. Numerous other facilities suffered substantial loss of roofing materials including the Lamar County Multi-Purpose Center. The tornado continued eastward downing numerous trees and causing widespread damage to roofs and outbuildings before dissipating just east of US Highway 11. The tornado at maximum width was estimated at 600 yards, had a track length of 13.4 miles, and had a maximum estimated intensity of 135 mph, EF-2.

This tornado began in northern Wilkinson County, crossing into far southeastern Adams County, then tracking across southern Franklin County, central Lincoln County, and into western Lawrence County before lifting on the south side of Monticello. The tornado also passed near the communities of Garden City, West Lincoln, Bogue Chitto, Enterprise, East Lincoln, and Harmony. The first instance of EF3 damage was an area of nearly complete devastation to mixed forest, including some instances of debarking, east of the Garden City community. A second area of EF3 damage was identified in western Lincoln County near Jackson Liberty Dr, where two truss transmission towers were damaged, including one bent to the ground and another collapsed. A third corridor of EF3 damage occurred along US Hwy 51 between Brookhaven and Bogue Chitto. In this area, tree damage was extensive with some debarking, a home was heavily damaged with exterior walls collapsed, and a mobile home park was devastated with debris and vehicles rowed along a center vortex path. Additional heavy tree damage occurred along the entire path. There were also multiple instances of EF2 structural damage in Franklin, Lincoln, and Lawrence counties.

Preliminary ground survey confirms EF1 damage beginning along US Highway 98 between New Augusta and Beaumont, with multiple areas of softwood and hardwood trees snapped or uprooted. Two concentrated areas of tree damage were noted along US Highway 98, suggesting multiple subvortices within a broader circulation. The maximum intensity occurred around US Highway 98 with winds around 100 mph. The tornado continued generally eastward, crossing Highway 198 and tracking along or near V L Toussaint Blvd before weakening over the north side of Beaumont. Farther southwest, sporadic tree damage was also observed along Highway 29 south of New Augusta. Satellite data will be examined over the coming weeks to determine the starting point for the tornado, as radar suggests that it likely formed over a wooded area southwest of New Augusta. Further track adjustments are likely once satellite data is available.

Preliminary ground survey confirms low end EF1 damage northeast of Grand Bay, Alabama. Maximum intensity of 88 mph was found on Jim McNeil Loop Road where several large tree limbs were down over a compact area. The tornado tracked southeast and produced sporadic damage on Dedicated Road where a horse trailer was flipped and more trees were damaged. After passing over Old Pascagoula Road, additional tree damage with downed limbs and snapped trees were found on Wells Road West. Satellite data will be examined in the coming weeks to confirm the starting point of the tornado, which is likely further to the northwest in wooded areas over Jackson Creek east of Grand Bay Wilmer Road.
